evidence: `backend/src/constants.ts` aggregates unrelated concerns (socket events, queue names, collection names, statuses, VoiceBot storage/env config) in one high-fan-in module (`review_context.import_graph_summary.top_imported` shows 23 importers), creating a cross-domain dependency hub.
`backend/src/constants.ts:355-368` and `:523-533` compute environment-derived values at module load time, so imports trigger runtime configuration decisions outside composition roots.

Auth/permission path duplicates identity loading across modules: `backend/src/api/middleware/auth.ts:87-114` fetches performer and permissions, `backend/src/api/middleware/roleGuard.ts:45-58` fetches performer again, and `backend/src/permissions/permission-manager.ts:202-227` fetches performer again.

`backend/src/services/db.ts:84-99` exposes runtime-scope extension points (`applyRuntimeScopeToAggregatePipeline`, `patchRuntimeTagIntoSetOnInsert`, `createRuntimeScopedCollectionProxy`) that are currently no-ops, while still presenting a proxy-based architecture (`:101-114`) as if scoping is active.

Index/bootstrap policy still depends on `runtime_tag` keys in `backend/src/constants.ts:149-167`, while DB layer exports runtime-scoping primitives that do not enforce any scope, producing unclear ownership of isolation policy.

`backend/src/api/routes/crm/codex.ts` combines HTTP transport, process orchestration (`spawn`), sync-retry policy, JSON parsing, and repo-path resolution in one route module (`:45-285`, `:291-402`), mixing infra adapter and API layer responsibilities.

Route path naming drifts heavily inside one router: `/taskTypes` (camelCase), `/project_groups` and `/project_tree` (snake_case), and `/bot-commands` (kebab-case) in `backend/src/api/routes/crm/index.ts`.
VoiceBot router keeps mixed-casing endpoint style (`/LLMGate`) while sibling endpoints are lowercase, and mixes singular/plural path semantics (`/upload` in CRM vs `/uploads` aliasing in VoiceBot).
All three hubs use different outward conventions for route composition (`crm/index.ts` explicit prefixes vs `finops/index.ts` and `voicebot/index.ts` heavy root mounts).
`sessionsSharedUtils.ts` normalizers frequently collapse invalid input to empty/null (`toTaskText`, `normalizeLinkedMessageRef`, `toObjectIdOrNull`) with no typed error path or diagnostics.

`sessionUrlUtils.ts` silently rewrites misconfigured legacy hosts and accepts any `VOICE_WEB_INTERFACE_URL` string without URL validation, producing fallback behavior rather than explicit configuration failure.

Across these shared utilities, failure signaling style is inconsistent: invalid input is sometimes preserved (`normalizeDateField` returns original trimmed string), sometimes dropped (`''`/`null`), making downstream error handling unpredictable.

Router integration seams are inconsistent: `crm/index.ts` maps each child to explicit prefixes while `finops/index.ts` mounts many children at `'/'`, pushing boundary semantics into child modules and obscuring ownership at this seam.

`voicebot/index.ts` combines flat root mounts and legacy alias mounts in the same hub; this creates implicit overlap risk and makes lifecycle/deprecation boundaries hard to reason about at integration points.

Shared utility seams in `sessionsSharedUtils.ts` encode multiple legacy payload shapes (`id/task_id/title/name/reference`, `_id/id/key`) directly in generic converters, indicating adapter logic is spread rather than isolated per boundary.

No-op runtime query wrappers are duplicated as pass-through indirection in multiple files (`voicebot/uploads.ts:38-40`, `voicebot/transcription.ts:17-21`, `voicebot/messageHelpers.ts:51-54`) without adding validation, policy, or translation.

Multiple routes re-implement near-identical coercion/parsing helpers instead of sharing typed boundary utilities (`crm/tickets.ts:51-69`, `crm/projects.ts:22-33`, `finops/expensesOperations.ts:17-51`, `finops/monthClosures.ts:10-20`).

`voicebot/sessions.ts` contains local utility ecosystems and alias adapters (`sessions.ts:402-417`, `sessions.ts:965-1010`) in the same file as endpoint orchestration, which increases abstraction surface without clear ownership boundaries.
`voicebot/sessions.ts` is a 6001-line route module with dozens of handlers and mixed concerns, forcing dense local control flow and high branch surface in one file.

`crm/legacy/performerspayments.ts` (815 lines) mixes analytics computation, DB queries, Google Drive integration, and document workflow orchestration in route handlers (`performerspayments.ts:38+`, `24-32`), creating long imperative internals.
Large handlers repeatedly inline normalization/filter logic instead of composing focused local units (`voicebot/sessions.ts:1545-1652`, `1672-1794`; `crm/tickets.ts` long handler set from `205+`).

Auth middlewares that serve similar API boundaries use different identity validation and failure semantics: `requireRole` and `requirePermission` both call `new ObjectId(user.userId)` without pre-validation, so malformed IDs surface as 500s instead of contract-level 4xx rejections.
`requireRole` filters `is_banned` while `requirePermission` does not, so users can be denied in one guarded route family and still pass another.
403 payload shapes differ across sibling auth boundaries (`allowed_roles` + human names vs `required_permissions` + permission keys), making client-side error handling inconsistent.

Several session mutation endpoints update records directly without any access check (`/update_name`, `/update_access_level`, `/update_dialogue_tag`, `/update_participants`, `/update_allowed_users`) in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts` around lines 2396-2759, while neighboring endpoints in the same file use `sessionAccessUtils.resolve` (`/update_project` at lines 2446-2452).

`/public_attachment/:session_id/:file_unique_id` in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/uploads.ts` (lines 910-965) is unauthenticated and does not call `checkSessionAccess`, unlike `/message_attachment/:message_id/:attachment_index` (lines 842-875) which enforces permission + session access.
holistic_context.structure shows `backend/src/api/routes/crm/` importing `backend/src/api/routes/crm/legacy/` 7 times, so legacy code is not isolated and acts as a live dependency boundary.
`backend/src/api/routes/crm/customers.ts` imports `./legacy/projecttreeaudit.js`, pulling active CRM behavior from legacy namespace.

`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/messageHelpers.ts` is route-adjacent but contains non-HTTP DB/domain normalization helpers (`ensureMessageCanonicalTranscription`, cleanup payload builders), indicating service logic placement drift inside routes.

Large UI orchestrators combine unrelated concerns in one component: `app/src/pages/voice/SessionsListPage.tsx` (1725 LOC) mixes URL state persistence, localStorage hydration, FAB polling/event sync, bulk-merge/delete workflows, and table render definitions; `app/src/components/crm/CRMKanban.tsx` (1394 LOC) similarly combines filtering, routing, edit flows, and table behavior.

Voice worker handlers duplicate a near-identical processor execution template instead of using a shared abstraction: `backend/src/workers/voicebot/handlers/customPrompt.ts` and `questionsHandler.ts` both do message/session lookup, categorization gating, OpenAI call, processor state writes, and parallel error plumbing with slightly different field names.

`backend/src/workers/voicebot/handlers/transcribeHandler.ts` centralizes too many responsibilities in one flow (transport recovery, split/segment transcription, retry policy, codex task side-effect, categorization enqueue, websocket update emission), while `processingLoop.ts` and `ingressHandlers.ts` also coordinate adjacent lifecycle concerns, increasing coupling.

FinOps table modules reimplement the same structural shell (pinned-month persistence, month-ordering, similar column builder patterns, repeated summary-cell cast workaround) in `app/src/components/PlanFactGrid.tsx`, `BonusesGrid.tsx`, and `ExpensesGrid.tsx`.

Helper logic is reimplemented in multiple places with near-identical behavior (`toObjectIdOrNull` and `runtimeQuery` clones in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessionsSharedUtils.ts` and `backend/src/workers/voicebot/handlers/transcribeHandler.ts`, plus a parallel `runtimeQuery` in `processingLoop.ts`).

Error-response boilerplate is duplicated in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/uploads.ts` (`uploadAnyWithErrorHandling` and `uploadImageWithErrorHandling`), repeating nearly identical Multer error branching and payload shaping instead of one shared adapter.
Legacy-alias forwarding in `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts` (`registerPostAlias` + many alias registrations) increases mechanical code volume without removing old surface area.

`runtime_tag` remains in active index definitions and runtime filters (`backend/src/constants.ts`, `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts`, `backend/src/workers/voicebot/handlers/processingLoop.ts`, `backend/src/workers/voicebot/handlers/transcribeHandler.ts`) even though runtime-tag routing is marked deprecated.

VoiceBot still exposes and wires legacy endpoint families in parallel with canonical flat routes (`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/index.ts` mounts `/sessions`, `/uploads`, `/transcription`, `/persons`, `/permissions`, `/LLMGate`; `sessions.ts` and `uploads.ts` both still serve `/close_session`).

CRM paths still depend on legacy compatibility layers (`backend/src/api/routes/crm/projects.ts` imports `./legacy/projecttreeaudit.js`; `backend/src/api/routes/crm/tickets.ts` still resolves `ticket_id` fallback path as legacy join input).

findings.summary: A single constants hub couples CRM/FinOps/VoiceBot/runtime concerns and amplifies change blast radius.
evidence: `backend/src/constants.ts:10-534` contains socket events, queue names, DB collection names, business statuses, VoiceBot jobs, and storage config in one file.

`backend/src/services/db.ts:11` and `backend/src/permissions/permission-manager.ts:11` both import constants from this monolith, forcing low-level services and permission logic to depend on broad cross-domain definitions.
findings.suggestion: Split constants by bounded context and layer (`domain/*`, `infra/*`, `voicebot/*`, `db/*`), keep only small re-export façades where needed, and stop using a single global constants entrypoint for unrelated modules.

findings.summary: Import-time environment evaluation in shared modules introduces hidden runtime coupling.
evidence: `backend/src/constants.ts:355-368` computes `VOICEBOT_QUEUES` from `process.env.APP_ENV` during module evaluation.
`backend/src/constants.ts:523-533` resolves file-size/storage defaults from env at import time.
`backend/src/utils/logger.ts:95-99` auto-initializes logger on first import chain call, and init path mutates filesystem (`:20-24`).
`backend/src/api/routes/crm/codex.ts:194-212` resolves binary/workdir from env in route module helpers, further spreading env-coupled behavior into import-heavy paths.
findings.suggestion: Move env reads and side-effectful setup to explicit bootstrap factories (e.g., `buildRuntimeConfig()`, `createLogger(config)`), inject config into modules, and keep shared imports pure/data-only.

findings.summary: Auth, role guard, and permission middleware repeat performer loading and diverge request contracts.
evidence: `auth.ts:25-43` defines one `AuthenticatedRequest` shape with required `user/performer/db`.
`roleGuard.ts:17-26` defines a second request shape with optional `user/db` and independently reloads performer (`:45-50`).
`permission-manager.ts:165-178` defines a third request shape and reloads performer in `requirePermission` (`:202-206`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single shared auth context type and one performer-resolution middleware stage; downstream guards should consume that context instead of re-querying DB.

findings.summary: DB runtime-scoping architecture is represented as layered proxy abstractions but currently behaves as pass-through.
evidence: `db.ts:84-87`, `:89-93`, and `:95-99` export scoping helpers that return unchanged values/collections.
`db.ts:101-114` wraps DB in proxy but delegates collection unchanged (`:108`).
Despite no-op runtime scoping in DB layer, index policy still encodes `runtime_tag` in startup indexes (`constants.ts:149-167`), so responsibility for isolation is ambiguous.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the proxy/scoping façade until real behavior exists, or implement real scoped behavior and enforce it consistently; align index strategy and runtime isolation policy under one explicit owner module.

findings.summary: The CRM Codex route owns process execution and sync orchestration, collapsing API and infrastructure boundaries.
evidence: `codex.ts:45-180` implements command lifecycle, timeout kill logic, retry flow, and sync remediation in-route.
`codex.ts:182-285` adds command parsing and execution-context resolution logic inside the route module.
Route returns ad-hoc payloads directly (`codex.ts:291-402`) instead of reusing shared response/error middleware conventions from `response.ts` and `error.ts`.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `CodexIssuesService` (command adapter + retry policy + parsing), keep route handlers thin (validation + mapping), and normalize route output through shared response/error envelope helpers.

findings.summary: Route naming conventions drift across and within hubs, creating non-deterministic API style.
evidence: `crm/index.ts` exposes mixed styles in one surface: `/taskTypes`, `/project_groups`, `/project_tree`, `/bot-commands`.
`voicebot/index.ts` includes uppercase route segment `/LLMGate` unlike lowercase siblings.
Hub-level path semantics differ (`/upload` in CRM, `/uploads` in VoiceBot aliases), reinforcing inconsistent public contract style.
findings.suggestion: Define one route-style convention (prefer lowercase kebab-case), codify it in a shared router-style guideline, and normalize hub mount paths + aliases through a planned compatibility window with explicit deprecation markers.

findings.summary: Sibling route hubs use different composition protocols, creating style islands in entrypoints.
evidence: `crm/index.ts` mostly uses explicit namespace mounting per module.
`finops/index.ts` mounts multiple independent routers at `'/'`, relying on child internals for segmentation.
`voicebot/index.ts` mixes both flat mounts and prefixed legacy mounts in one file.
findings.suggestion: Standardize hub composition to one protocol (explicit module prefixes or explicit documented flat contract) and apply consistently across all route hubs to reduce entrypoint-level drift.

findings.summary: Shared normalization utilities silently absorb malformed values instead of exposing explicit error outcomes.
evidence: `toTaskText` returns empty string for unsupported types; `normalizeLinkedMessageRef` returns empty string for non-string values.
`toObjectIdOrNull` drops invalid IDs to null without a reason code.
`active()` in `sessionUrlUtils` silently rewrites legacy host and does no URL validity check on configured base.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed parse results for boundary helpers (e.g., `{ok,value}|{ok:false,code,details}`) and add config validation for `VOICE_WEB_INTERFACE_URL` at startup so malformed inputs fail predictably.

findings.summary: Date normalization mixes preservation and nulling strategies, making invalid-value handling unpredictable.
evidence: `normalizeDateField` returns original trimmed string when parse fails, but returns `null` for empty values and ISO for parseable values.
Other helpers in the same module collapse invalid values to empty/null, so consumers cannot rely on a uniform invalid-input contract.
No companion error metadata is returned to distinguish intentionally preserved raw values from parsing failures.
findings.suggestion: Use one invalid-value contract across shared utils (either strict rejection or explicit tagged raw/parsed variants) and keep it consistent across all normalizers used at route boundaries.

findings.summary: Legacy shape translation is embedded in generic shared utils instead of isolated at explicit module boundaries.
evidence: `toTaskReferenceList` accepts multiple heterogeneous key names (`id/task_id/title/name/reference`) in one generic helper.
`toIdString` recursively accepts `_id/id/key`, mixing distinct external/internal identity conventions.
Hub routers simultaneously maintain flat and legacy alias paths, so boundary adaptation is occurring in multiple places instead of one clear adapter layer.
findings.suggestion: Move legacy-shape adaptation into dedicated ingress adapters per route family (CRM/VoiceBot) and keep shared utils strict and schema-driven to reduce cross-boundary ambiguity.

findings.summary: VoiceBot hub combines canonical and legacy routes without an explicit seam contract, increasing coupling and ambiguity.
evidence: `voicebot/index.ts` mounts canonical flat routes and legacy aliases in parallel (`/`, `/sessions`, `/uploads`, `/LLMGate`).
No explicit precedence/deprecation policy exists in code-level API composition to indicate lifespan or conflict rules.
`finops/index.ts` demonstrates similar implicitness by stacking many routers at `'/'`, making collision analysis dependent on child-route internals.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit adapter routers for legacy paths with deprecation telemetry and keep canonical routers isolated; avoid mixed mounting styles in one hub to clarify handoff ownership.

findings.summary: Runtime query helpers are repeated as no-op pass-through wrappers, adding indirection without policy value.
evidence: `uploads.ts:38-40` defines `runtimeSessionQuery`/`runtimeMessageQuery` as identity functions.
`transcription.ts:17-21` repeats the same identity wrappers.
`messageHelpers.ts:51-54` exports the same identity wrappers again.
findings.suggestion: Replace file-local identity wrappers with one shared runtime-filter utility (or remove wrappers entirely where no filter is required) so runtime policy is explicit and not duplicated.

findings.summary: Input coercion helpers are reimplemented per route module with slight signature drift, indicating missing shared boundary abstraction.
evidence: `tickets.ts:51-69` defines local `toNonEmptyString` and `toObjectId`.
`projects.ts:22-33` defines different local `toRecord`/`toObjectId` semantics.
`expensesOperations.ts:17-51` and `monthClosures.ts:10-20` duplicate month parsing/validation logic with separate helper names.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared typed request parsers (Zod-backed or equivalent) for common primitives (`ObjectId`, month strings, non-empty text) and reuse them across route modules.

findings.summary: Sibling route modules expose inconsistent response/error contracts, making integration seams unpredictable.
evidence: `auth.ts` and FinOps routes use `sendOk`/`AppError` flow.
`crm/finances.ts` and `crm/projects.ts` return direct `res.status(...).json(...)` with varying payload shapes.
`voicebot/llmgate.ts` uses `success/data/error/timestamp` envelope unrelated to other route conventions.
findings.suggestion: Define one route-boundary contract per API family (or globally), and enforce it via shared responder helpers and schema-typed handler wrappers.

findings.summary: Voicebot session access checks are implemented in multiple local variants instead of one policy seam.
evidence: `transcription.ts:42-62` defines `hasSessionReadAccess` with local permission checks.
`uploads.ts` introduces separate `checkSessionAccess` flow (declared around `327+`).
`sessions.ts` uses a separate `sessionAccessUtils.resolve` path (`1688-1692`), resulting in at least three access seams.
findings.suggestion: Extract a single voicebot session access service with canonical read/update predicates and consume it from all routes to prevent drift.

findings.summary: Very large route files combine unrelated responsibilities, producing dense control flow and brittle local internals.
evidence: `sessions.ts` is 6001 lines and mixes alias routing (`402-417`), permissions, session CRUD, CRM sync, project file APIs, event retries, and transcript/categorization mutations.
`performerspayments.ts` is 815 lines and mixes DB analytics, payment calculation, and Google Drive document workflows (`38+`, `24-32`).
`tickets.ts` (686 lines) combines read/list aggregation, status mutation, comments, and work-hours migration compatibility paths in one module.
findings.suggestion: Split each route file by cohesive use-cases (query/read, mutation, external integration, compatibility aliases) and move heavy orchestration into dedicated service modules.

findings.summary: Auth middleware converts user IDs to ObjectId without validating input shape first.
evidence: `roleGuard.ts` uses `new ObjectId(user.userId)` at line 47 without `ObjectId.isValid` guard.
`permission-manager.ts` uses `new ObjectId(user.userId)` at line 204 without prior validation.
A malformed `userId` from token parsing or legacy rows throws and is caught as 500, producing inconsistent API behavior for authentication failures.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared auth ID parser (e.g., `parsePerformerObjectId`) used by both middlewares. Return a deterministic 401/400 response for invalid identifiers instead of allowing constructor exceptions to fall into generic 500 handling.

findings.summary: Two primary access guards enforce different account-state and error-response contracts.
evidence: `roleGuard.ts` includes `is_banned: { $ne: true }` in performer lookup (line 49).
`permission-manager.ts` performer lookup omits `is_banned` (lines 203-206).
403 responses differ in schema and semantics (`allowed_roles` + role names vs `required_permissions`), forcing clients to branch on middleware-specific payloads.
findings.suggestion: Define a single shared performer-loading + denial-response contract for middleware boundaries (common banned/deleted checks and normalized error envelope fields) and reuse it in both guards.

findings.summary: Contextual permission checks use inconsistent session ID validation paths.
evidence: `checkSessionOwnership` validates `session_id` with `ObjectId.isValid` before DB query (lines 314-320).
`checkProjectAssignment` uses `new ObjectId(sessionId)` without validity guard (lines 344-348), creating throw-based control flow for malformed IDs.
The seam handles equivalent request context with two incompatible error paths, which complicates middleware composition and observability.
findings.suggestion: Centralize request ID extraction + validation for `session_id`/`project_id` into one utility that returns typed parse results, then consume it from all contextual checks and role/permission middlewares.

findings.summary: Audit logging seam bypasses typed auth context and reinterprets request identity locally.
evidence: `projecttreeaudit.ts` accepts plain `Request` and casts it to a local shape with optional `user/performer` (lines 52-56).
A canonical `AuthenticatedRequest` type already exists in `permission-manager.ts` (lines 165-178), but this logger does not use it.
Local casting creates weak coupling where actor identity fields can drift silently from middleware-populated request contracts.
findings.suggestion: Use a shared request auth-context type (or a small `resolveRequesterId(req)` utility in a common module) so audit logging and permission middleware consume the same identity seam.

findings.summary: Multiple session mutation endpoints bypass per-session authorization entirely.
evidence: `sessions.ts` updates session records in `/update_name` (2396-2421), `/update_access_level` (2536-2555), `/update_dialogue_tag` (2565-2588), `/update_participants` (2665-2716), and `/update_allowed_users` (2726-2759) without calling `sessionAccessUtils.resolve` or checking permissions against the target session.
A sibling endpoint in the same file (`/update_project`, 2446-2452) correctly performs session-level access validation, proving this is inconsistent rather than intentional.
`uploads.ts` contains an explicit reusable access gate (`checkSessionAccess`, 327-385), further highlighting missing gate usage in the listed session handlers.
findings.suggestion: Require a single session-level authorization guard for all session mutations: validate `session_id`, resolve session via shared helper, enforce required permission tier (`UPDATE`/`DELETE` as applicable), and only then execute writes. Apply this uniformly to all mutation routes in `sessions.ts`.

findings.summary: Public attachment endpoint allows file retrieval without session authorization checks.
evidence: `uploads.ts` exposes `router.get('/public_attachment/:session_id/:file_unique_id', publicAttachmentHandler)` (965) with no `PermissionManager.requirePermission` middleware.
`publicAttachmentHandler` (910-963) fetches and serves files by `(session_id, file_unique_id)` without `checkSessionAccess`.
By contrast, `message_attachment` route (842-875) requires `VOICEBOT_SESSIONS.READ_OWN` and explicitly calls `checkSessionAccess`, showing a stricter model exists but is bypassed by the public path.
findings.suggestion: Either protect `public_attachment` with the same auth + `checkSessionAccess` flow as `message_attachment`, or issue short-lived signed URLs scoped to actor/session instead of direct stable IDs.

findings.summary: Authorization seam is duplicated across route modules, causing behavioral drift and uneven hardening.
evidence: `sessions.ts` defines `sessionAccessUtils.resolve` (1088-1121) while `uploads.ts` defines `checkSessionAccess` (327-385); both encode similar ownership/public/restricted logic separately.

Handler integration is inconsistent: some routes compose through shared resolver (`sessions.ts` `/update_project`), others embed custom logic (`/save_create_tasks`, `/delete`, `/send_to_crm`) or no logic at all (`/update_name`, `/update_access_level`, `/update_dialogue_tag`, `/update_participants`, `/update_allowed_users`).
This seam fragmentation directly enabled missed checks and route-by-route policy divergence.
findings.suggestion: Extract one canonical `resolveVoicebotSessionAccess` service (permission + session visibility + reason code) and require all session/attachment handlers to call it through a thin middleware or helper wrapper; remove ad-hoc authorization branches from individual handlers.

findings.summary: Active CRM routes depend on `crm/legacy` internals, so legacy is functioning as primary package space.
evidence: `customers.ts` imports `./legacy/projecttreeaudit.js`, coupling current CRUD flows to legacy folder internals.
Blind packet coupling matrix reports `backend/src/api/routes/crm/ → backend/src/api/routes/crm/legacy/` = 7 edges, which is too high for a supposed migration-only namespace.
Legacy files (`projectgroups.ts`, `botcommands.ts`) still expose active production endpoints, not compatibility stubs.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `backend/src/api/routes/crm/project-tree/` and move `projecttreeaudit` there as canonical module. 2) Move active legacy routes into purpose folders (`crm/config/botcommands.ts`, `crm/payments/performersPayments.ts`, `crm/project-groups/projectGroups.ts`) and keep `crm/legacy/*` as thin re-export adapters for one release. 3) Update imports in `customers.ts` and sibling routes to new canonical paths. 4) Validate with `rg -n "crm/legacy|legacy/projecttreeaudit" backend/src/api/routes/crm` (should only show adapters), then `cd backend && npm run build`.

findings.summary: One route module owns analytics, document generation, and external Drive orchestration at once.
evidence: `performerspayments.ts` imports finance/stat libs (`dayjs`, lodash) and document infra (`googleapis`, `number-to-words-ru`) and executes both concerns in route handlers.
The same file handles DB aggregation (`WORK_HOURS`, `TASKS`) and document side effects (Google Docs/Drive copy, folder operations), signaling mixed ownership.
`dictionary.ts` repeats the same pattern by acting as an aggregation hub for unrelated dictionaries (tree, performers, task taxonomy, epics, income types) in one route-level module.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `backend/src/services/crm/payments/` with `financeStats.ts` and `actDocumentService.ts`; keep route file as orchestration-only (`req/res` + validation + service calls). 2) Create `backend/src/services/crm/dictionaries/` and split dictionary loaders by bounded context (`projectTreeLoader`, `taskTypeLoader`, `epicsLoader`, `incomeTypeLoader`). 3) Move code in this order: pure transforms first, then external API calls, then route thin wrappers. 4) Update imports and run `cd backend && npm run build` plus targeted grep checks `rg -n "google\.docs|google\.drive" backend/src/api/routes` (should no longer match route files).

findings.summary: Voicebot `routes/` folder contains reusable domain/DB helper module, not just transport adapters.
evidence: `messageHelpers.ts` exports DB/domain operations (`ensureMessageCanonicalTranscription`, categorization cleanup builders) with no Express router surface.
`llmgate.ts` embeds OpenAI client lifecycle and provider mapping directly in a route module, mixing transport and external-service concerns in package placement.
Directory profile shows `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/` as a 10-file high-LOC area (9088 LOC) with broad fan-out (avg_fan_out 5.4), consistent with route layer absorbing service responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Create `backend/src/services/voicebot/messages/` and move `messageHelpers.ts` logic there (`messageCanonicalization.ts`, `categorizationCleanup.ts`). 2) Create `backend/src/services/voicebot/llm/llmGateClient.ts` and keep `llmgate.ts` as request validation + response mapping only. 3) Update route imports to new services. 4) Validate with `rg -n "ensureMessageCanonicalTranscription|new OpenAI\(" backend/src/api/routes/voicebot` (route hits should reduce to wrappers), then `cd backend && npm run build`.

findings.summary: Voicebot hub exposes dual legacy and flat mount surfaces, preserving migration state as permanent architecture.
evidence: `index.ts` mounts both flat routes and legacy aliases (`/sessions`, `/uploads`, `/LLMGate`), so responsibility is split between canonical and compatibility pathing.
Alias casing (`/LLMGate`) differs from modern lowercase conventions and keeps legacy naming at primary boundary.
The same hub mixes compatibility and canonical mounts in one layer instead of isolating compatibility adapters.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) Introduce explicit `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/legacy/index.ts` that owns all compatibility aliases. 2) Keep `voicebot/index.ts` canonical-only (`session_done`, upload, persons, permissions, transcription, llmgate under normalized lowercase path). 3) Route compatibility traffic through the legacy router with deprecation logging and expiry date. 4) Update import wiring and validate with `rg -n "LLMGate|/sessions|/uploads" backend/src/api/routes/voicebot` and `cd backend && npm run build`.

findings.summary: Sessions list page mixes routing, persistence, orchestration, and rendering in one monolith
evidence: `SessionsListPage.tsx` contains URL query schema, localStorage restore/persist, FAB sync interval/event wiring, bulk delete/merge mutations, and full table column/render logic in one component body.

`MeetingCard.tsx` and `PossibleTasks.tsx` repeat adjacent orchestration concerns (session-side effects, tag persistence, task mutation flows), indicating weak ownership boundaries in the voice UI layer.
findings.suggestion: Split voice session UI into domain hooks/modules: `useSessionListQueryState`, `useFabSessionSync`, `useBulkSessionActions`, and a pure `SessionsTable` view. Keep `MeetingCard`/`PossibleTasks` focused on presentation plus narrow action adapters, moving persistence and polling logic into shared hooks.

findings.summary: Prompt processor handlers duplicate the same execution skeleton with drift-prone variations
evidence: `customPrompt.ts` and `questionsHandler.ts` both implement identical lifecycle phases: validate ids, load message/session, skip on empty categorization, OpenAI request, write processor success state, write processor failure state, log result.
Field naming and update paths differ (`processors_data.<name>.*` vs mixed `questioning_*` fields), showing copy-and-modify evolution instead of a single processor contract.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared processor executor (e.g., `runMessageProcessor`) that standardizes load/guard/update/error flow and accepts strategy callbacks for prompt/instructions + output normalization. Unify persisted processor field layout across custom prompts and questioning.

findings.summary: Transcribe handler combines transport, DSP, policy, tasking, and realtime side-effects
evidence: `handleTranscribeJob` performs Telegram file recovery, fallback text path, dedup reuse-by-hash, large-file splitting/transcription, retry/quota policy, session corruption state writes, codex task creation, categorization enqueue, and socket event emission in one long routine.
Adjacent modules (`processingLoop.ts`, `ingressHandlers.ts`) also contain lifecycle/retry orchestration, making behavior spread across files with overlapping responsibilities.
findings.suggestion: Refactor into a pipeline with explicit stages: `resolveAudioSource`, `transcribeContent`, `persistTranscriptionResult`, `runPostTranscriptionHooks` (codex/categorization/events), and `applyRetryPolicy`. Keep `handleTranscribeJob` as a coordinator over typed stage results.

findings.summary: FinOps grids duplicate month-pinning and table-scaffold mechanics across modules
evidence: All three grids implement similar pinned-month localStorage handling, ordered month composition, per-month column generation, and summary-cell type casting.
Each file carries bespoke but structurally equivalent table shell logic, increasing change cost for shared behavior updates (pinning, month header UX, summary formatting).
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared `FinancialMonthTable` scaffold/hook (`usePinnedMonths`, `buildOrderedMonthColumns`, summary helpers) and keep each grid responsible only for domain row mapping and cell render specifics.

findings.summary: UI stores combine multiple modal/workflow domains, creating broad mutable surfaces
evidence: `sessionsUIStore.ts` bundles participant modal, access users modal, categorization selection, two sort preferences, tickets modal editing state, and title-generation workflow in one store.
`projectsStore.ts` and `permissionsStore.ts` similarly merge fetch lifecycle, cache policy, mutation side-effects, and UI messaging concerns, expanding blast radius of local changes.
findings.suggestion: Split stores into narrower domain slices (`sessionParticipantsUI`, `sessionTicketsUI`, `sessionSortPrefs`, `projectsDataStore`, `permissionsAdminStore`) and move notification side-effects into service/adaptor helpers to reduce cross-feature coupling.

findings.summary: Deprecated `runtime_tag` isolation is still part of active query/index logic across routes and workers.
evidence: `backend/src/constants.ts` still defines startup indexes keyed by `runtime_tag` (`TASKS_IS_DELETED_TASK_STATUS_RUNTIME_TAG`, `WORK_HOURS_TICKET_DB_ID_RUNTIME_TAG`).
`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts` repeatedly applies `mergeWithRuntimeFilter(..., { field: 'runtime_tag', ... })` and persists `runtime_tag` in write payloads.
`processingLoop.ts` and `transcribeHandler.ts` both define `runtimeQuery` wrappers over `mergeWithRuntimeFilter` with `field: 'runtime_tag'`.
`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/uploads.ts` still reads and emits `session.runtime_tag` in socket payloads.
findings.suggestion: Complete the runtime isolation cutover by removing `runtime_tag` from active filters/index contracts and replacing remaining selectors with environment/database-level isolation only; keep read-only backward compatibility in dedicated migration scripts rather than hot paths.

findings.summary: Canonical and legacy VoiceBot APIs are still exposed simultaneously, leaving migration in an unfinished steady state.
evidence: `backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/index.ts` mounts flat routers (`'/'`) and also mounts legacy prefixed routers (`'/sessions'`, `'/uploads'`, `'/transcription'`, `'/persons'`, `'/permissions'`, `'/LLMGate'`).
`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/sessions.ts` still registers multiple POST aliases via `registerPostAlias(...)`, including `'/close_session' -> '/session_done'`.
`backend/src/api/routes/voicebot/uploads.ts` independently still implements `POST /close_session`, creating duplicate close handlers in different routers.
findings.suggestion: Constrain legacy paths to a single compatibility gateway with explicit sunset controls (feature flag + telemetry + removal date), and route all close-session semantics through one backend handler to avoid split behavior.

findings.summary: Active CRM routes still depend on legacy modules and legacy identifier fallbacks, indicating unfinished domain normalization.
evidence: `backend/src/api/routes/crm/projects.ts` imports and uses `./legacy/projecttreeaudit.js` from a non-legacy mainline route.
`backend/src/api/routes/crm/tickets.ts` still performs legacy `ticket_id` reconciliation (`legacyTicketId` fallback lookup by `{ id }` and optional `{ _id }`).
findings.suggestion: Extract legacy adapters into an explicit migration boundary (single normalization module), and make mainline routes operate only on canonical IDs/types after adapter conversion.

findings.summary: Small utility primitives are copied across modules instead of shared, increasing mechanical drift and maintenance cost.
evidence: `toObjectIdOrNull` is defined in `sessionsSharedUtils.ts` and redefined with equivalent behavior in `transcribeHandler.ts`.
`runtimeQuery` wrappers are separately declared in `transcribeHandler.ts` and `processingLoop.ts` with the same `mergeWithRuntimeFilter` configuration.
findings.suggestion: Create a small shared `voicebot/queryUtils` module for ID normalization and runtime query shaping, then delete local clones and import canonical helpers.

findings.summary: Uploads router contains copy-pasted Multer error-to-response adapters with only minor differences.
evidence: `uploadAnyWithErrorHandling` and `uploadImageWithErrorHandling` in `uploads.ts` duplicate the same error branching (`MulterError` size check, generic error mapping, default fallback).
The project already has centralized response/error middleware (`response.ts`, `error.ts`), but this route keeps bespoke in-route mapping logic.
findings.suggestion: Unify file-upload error mapping in one reusable helper/middleware (optionally integrated with `sendError`/`AppError`) and parameterize only size-limit and request-id behavior.

findings.summary: Most CRM routers are mounted without auth/role middleware, while only reports.ts enforces admin gating.
evidence: crm/index.ts mounts 18 subrouters but does not apply router.use(authMiddleware) or router.use(requireAdmin).
rg shows auth middleware usage in CRM only in reports.ts; tickets/projects/customers/import/uploads/legacy handlers are directly exposed.
findings.suggestion: Apply a consistent CRM authorization boundary in crm/index.ts (at least authMiddleware; requireAdmin or permission-based guard per route group). Then explicitly mark any intentional public CRM endpoints in code comments and tests.

findings.summary: Fund and plan-fact routes are unauthenticated while sibling finops routes enforce auth/admin checks.
evidence: fund.ts and planFact.ts handlers have no authMiddleware/requireAdmin in route definitions.
finops subroutes (expensesOperations.ts, monthClosures.ts, fxRates.ts, expensesCategories.ts) explicitly apply authMiddleware and requireAdmin for write operations.
findings.suggestion: Enforce auth on fund/plan-fact endpoints to match FinOps policy; if read-only access differs, encode it with explicit permission checks and centralized route-level middleware.

findings.summary: Route modules use incompatible success/error envelopes, producing inconsistent client failure semantics.
evidence: auth.ts and finops routes use sendOk/AppError contract, while CRM routes frequently return res.status(...).json({ error: String(error) }).
llmgate.ts returns { success: boolean, data/error } instead of { data, error } envelope used by response middleware.
findings.suggestion: Standardize all route responses on middleware response helpers and typed error codes; migrate legacy ad-hoc payloads behind a compatibility adapter only where external clients still require old shapes.

findings.summary: API surface mixes RPC-style POST endpoints and RESTful resources in the same backend, without a clear boundary contract.
evidence: CRM routes use POST for read/get/list/delete actions (e.g., /get-by-id, /list, /delete).
FinOps routes in same API use GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE resource semantics, causing mixed client expectations for idempotency and caching.
findings.suggestion: Define per-domain API style rules (RPC vs REST), document them, and migrate endpoints in batches with compatibility aliases and deprecation headers.

findings.summary: Sibling routes use conflicting path naming conventions (camelCase, snake_case, kebab-case, Pascal-like casing).
evidence: crm/index.ts mixes /taskTypes, /project_groups, /project_tree, /bot-commands, /performers-payments.
voicebot/index.ts mounts /LLMGate while llmgate.ts path is /run_prompt and other voicebot paths are lowercase.
findings.suggestion: Adopt one URL naming convention (kebab-case recommended), add route aliases for backward compatibility, and add a route lint check to block new nonconforming paths.

findings.summary: FinOps router composes parent-level fund/plan-fact modules, creating duplicated mount points and blurred module direction.
evidence: finops/index.ts imports ../fund.ts and ../planFact.ts and mounts them at '/'.
index.ts separately mounts fundRouter and planFactRouter directly, yielding two topologies for the same capabilities.
findings.suggestion: Move fund/plan-fact into dedicated finops submodules (or keep only one canonical mount path), then remove duplicate mounting and add contract tests for canonical endpoint paths.

findings.summary: Migration shims remain active as first-class API entry points, extending old/new contract overlap.
evidence: voicebot/index.ts mounts flat '/' and legacy '/sessions' '/uploads' '/LLMGate' aliases simultaneously.
sessions.ts registers alias behavior (sessions_in_crm/in_crm) while CRM proxy route also exposes restart_create_tasks and sessions_in_crm, duplicating integration path.
findings.suggestion: Publish a migration cutoff plan: keep one canonical endpoint per capability, convert duplicates to thin 301/410-compatible adapters with telemetry, then remove legacy mounts after client confirmation windows.

findings.summary: Legacy CRM package is still tightly coupled to active CRM routes, reducing navigability and ownership clarity.
evidence: holistic structure: crm/ has 14 files (3085 LOC) and crm/legacy has 6 files (1765 LOC), both marked production.
coupling matrix shows crm/ → crm/legacy import edge count 7, so legacy is not isolated.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) create `backend/src/api/routes/crm/tree/` and `backend/src/api/routes/crm/payments/` for still-active legacy logic; 2) move files in order: projecttree.ts/projectgroups.ts/projecttreeaudit.ts, then performerspayments.ts, then tasktypes.ts/botcommands.ts; 3) keep temporary re-export stubs in `legacy/` for one release; 4) update imports with `rg -n "crm/legacy" backend/src/api/routes/crm backend/src` and replace; 5) validate with `cd backend && npm run build && npm run test:parallel-safe`.

findings.summary: Runtime query helper abstractions currently do no transformation and only add call-site noise.
evidence: transcription.ts defines runtimeSessionQuery/runtimeMessageQuery as identity functions.
permissions.ts defines runtimePermissionsLogQuery(query) => query; wrappers are then threaded through find/findOne/update calls.
findings.suggestion: Either inline these calls now, or replace with one shared runtime-scope utility that enforces actual policy fields; avoid per-file identity wrappers with identical signatures.

findings.summary: Scaffolding artifacts from migration (placeholder wrappers and repetitive migration headers) remain in production routes without behavioral value.
evidence: Multiple files include large migration provenance comments but no linked removal criteria.
No-op runtime wrapper helpers and repeated boilerplate patterns suggest generated scaffolding left in place after transition.
findings.suggestion: Create a migration debt cleanup pass: remove no-op scaffolding, convert provenance comments into tracked changelog/doc links, and enforce a lint rule forbidding identity helper wrappers in route modules.

findings.summary: The sessions route file has become a subsystem-in-a-file, obscuring ownership and architectural boundaries.
evidence: sessions.ts is 6001 lines and combines endpoints for session listing/lifecycle, taskflow operations, categorization edits, socket queue side-effects, and compatibility aliases.
voicebot package profile totals 9088 LOC across 10 files, with one dominant file concentrating capability ownership.
findings.suggestion: Split sessions.ts by capability (`sessions.lifecycle.ts`, `sessions.taskflow.ts`, `sessions.categorization.ts`, `sessions.read.ts`) and keep index.ts as composition-only router wiring.

findings.summary: CRM-to-Voicebot integration is implemented as ad-hoc HTTP proxying at route layer, making boundary behavior inconsistent and hard to reason about.
evidence: crm/voicebot.ts manually forwards tokens from x-authorization/cookies and remaps response payloads/statuses via fetch.
Other integrations (reports) use direct service calls instead of route-to-route proxying, so seam strategy is inconsistent within CRM.
findings.suggestion: Replace route-to-route fetch proxy with a shared internal service function for restart/create-tasks and session reads; centralize auth context conversion once and call the service from both CRM and voicebot routers.

findings.summary: Request validation and payload normalization are repeatedly reimplemented per route, leading to structural drift and mixed behavior.
evidence: FinOps routes use local parser helpers and AppError patterns; CRM routes manually mutate req.body objects and ObjectId conversion logic per handler.
No common schema boundary is used across these sibling domains, so validation strictness and error details vary by file.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared route-boundary schemas (Zod) and mappers per domain (`crmSchemas`, `finopsSchemas`), then migrate handlers to parse once and pass typed payloads into service functions.
